meta.description: Universal prologue - all characters begin here after their origin cutscene

node_001.title: The Lower District
text: Your eyes open to a ceiling stained with rust and condensation. The hum of a faulty power relay vibrates through the thin walls of a room barely wide enough to stretch your arms. A cot, a metal shelf bolted to the wall, a cracked mirror. This is home. This is all you can afford in Sector 7 of the Lower District.

Rain hammers the single window. Through the grimy glass you can see the city stacked above you like a cage built on top of a cage. Flying transports cut through the downpour in organized lanes, their running lights smearing orange and white across the dark. Far above, the Cloud Towers rise past the storm layer into clean air and sunlight you haven't seen in months. Their spires pulse with slow, rhythmic light, broadcasting signal for the memory stack network that keeps the rich immortal.

A massive holographic advertisement flickers to life on the building across the street. A beautiful woman smiles, touching the back of her neck where a chrome port gleams. MERIDIAN STACK SERVICES, the text reads. WHY DIE WHEN YOU CAN TRANSFER? FINANCING AVAILABLE. The financing they don't mention would take you four lifetimes to pay off.

text.text: Your internal HUD boots up sluggishly, feeding you diagnostic data you didn't ask for. Ambient temperature, atmospheric toxin levels, the charge state of your prosthetics. The readout confirms what your body already knows: the air down here is slowly killing anyone without filters. Your augmentations give you maybe ten more years than the baseline humans sharing this squalor.

Your synthetic skin registers the humidity as a cascade of numerical data before your emotion-processing layer translates it into something like discomfort. You were built to serve in the towers above. How you ended up discarded in the slums is a question that surfaces every time you boot from sleep mode, and every time the answer file comes back corrupted.

You sit up and your horns scrape the low ceiling, leaving fresh grooves alongside dozens of old ones. The Lower District wasn't built for your kind. Nothing in this city was. The orcs who survived the Gene Riots were herded down here and forgotten, left to build lives in spaces designed for smaller bodies and smaller dreams.

The iron walls ache against your senses. Your ancestors lived centuries in forests that no longer exist, breathing air that no longer flows. Now you breathe recycled smog and count the years you have left on hands that were meant to outlive mountains. Elven longevity means nothing when the environment is engineered to shorten every breath.

You're baseline. No augments worth mentioning, no genetic legacy, no corporate sponsorship. In a city that prices immortality by the credit, being an ordinary human in the Lower District is its own kind of death sentence, just one that takes longer to execute.

choices.text: Get up and look out the window
Check the cracked mirror

node_002.title: The View Below
text: You press your face near the glass. Seven stories down, the street is a river of neon and shadow. Vendors have strung tarps between buildings to keep the acid rain off their stalls. People move in clusters, heads down, hoods up. Nobody lingers in the open. The patrol drones see to that.

Two AI enforcement drones glide past your window, their searchlights cutting white circles through the rain. They're Meridian Sec-Corp models, the ones with the crowd-dispersal tasers and facial recognition suites. They scan everyone below with mechanical patience, cataloging faces, checking stack registrations, flagging anomalies.

Below the drone line, a group of children dart between the stalls, quick as rats. One of them looks up at your window and flips an obscene gesture before vanishing into an alley. Life in the Lower District starts young and hard.

text.text: Your optic zoom engages automatically, pulling focus on the drones. Meridian Sec-7 chassis, last year's model. You can see the seams where their armor plates meet. You can also see the aftermarket signal jammer bolted to the building across the way, someone in this block is running interference on the drone net. Interesting.

The drones ping your wireless signature as they pass. You feel it as a cold tickle at the base of your skull. They're checking your registration. The forged papers that list you as a domestic service unit should hold, but each scan is a coin flip with your existence on one side.

choices.text: Check the cracked mirror before heading out
Get dressed and head for the street

node_003.title: The Cracked Mirror
text: The mirror is bolted to the wall at a height that assumes everyone is the same size. A crack runs diagonally through the glass, splitting your reflection into two slightly offset copies of the same tired face. You look older than you should. Everyone down here does.

On the shelf beneath the mirror sits the sum total of your worldly possessions: a battered datapad with a cracked screen, a few credit chips that wouldn't buy a meal in the mid-levels, and a knife with electrical tape wrapped around the handle where the grip rotted away.

text.text: In the mirror, the chrome of your augmentations catches the room's dim light. The prosthetics are functional but visibly old, the kind of hardware that was cutting-edge a decade ago. Hairline cracks web across the polymer casing of your left arm. You need maintenance you can't afford, from a technician you don't trust.

Your reflection stares back with amber eyes set in a face that the upper city would call monstrous. The tusks, the heavy brow, the grey-green skin. Down here, nobody looks twice. Down here, there are worse things to be than an orc.

Even in the Lower District, your features carry an echo of something older and finer than this place deserves. The angular cheekbones, the elongated ears, the eyes that hold light differently. But the smog has dulled your skin and thinned your hair. You look like a painting left in the rain.

text: You pocket the credit chips and the knife. The datapad buzzes once. A message notification blinks in the corner of the screen, but the sender field is blank. Just a location pin dropped on the map: Mako's Repair Shop, three blocks east.

choices.text: Read the anonymous message more carefully
Pocket the datapad and head outside

node_004.title: Leaving the Room
text: You pull on your jacket, a synthetic thing that's lost most of its weatherproofing, and push through the door into the corridor. The hallway smells of burnt cooking oil and recycled air. Exposed pipes run along the ceiling, sweating condensation. The floor is metal grating, and through the gaps you can see the level below, where someone is arguing in a language the city's translation software doesn't bother to support.

The stairwell at the end of the hall is lit by a single flickering tube. Graffiti covers every surface. STACK THE RICH. MERIDIAN LIES. IRON COLLECTIVE FOREVER. Someone has drawn a crude skull with circuit-board patterns and the words THE NETWORK SEES.

You descend seven flights. Each landing has its own micro-ecosystem of noise and smell. A couple screaming at each other. The chemical tang of a home narcotics lab. A baby crying. A televised stack-transfer ceremony playing at maximum volume, some corporate heir shedding their old body for a younger model to the sound of applause.
The lobby is a concrete box with a security gate that hasn't worked in years. Beyond it, the street.

choices.text: Step out into the rain

node_005.title: The Anonymous Message
text: You tap the notification. The message unfolds on the cracked screen, text only, no embedded media, no sender metadata. Whoever sent this knows how to stay invisible.
The message reads: COME TO MAKO'S. YOU DON'T KNOW ME YET BUT I KNOW WHAT YOU ARE. THE PEOPLE LOOKING FOR YOU WON'T ASK POLITELY. CLOCK IS TICKING.

Below the text, the location pin pulses red. Three blocks east. Mako's Repair Shop. You've passed it before, a cluttered storefront wedged between a noodle stand and a defunct stack clinic. Mako herself is a fixture of the neighborhood, a mechanic who fixes everything from prosthetic limbs to stolen drones, no questions asked.
The message deletes itself as you finish reading. The notification vanishes as if it never existed. Only the location pin remains, burning quietly on your map.

text.text: Your threat-assessment subroutine flags this at amber. Anonymous contact, self-deleting messages, urgency language. This is either a trap or a lifeline. In the Lower District, those are often the same thing.

You run a linguistic analysis on the message text. The syntax patterns suggest the author is educated, probably mid-level origin, deliberately writing below their register to blend in. Someone from above, reaching down.

choices.text: This changes things. Head out immediately.
Could be a trap. Pocket the datapad cautiously and leave.

node_006.title: The Street
text: The rain hits you the moment you step outside. It's warm and faintly chemical, the kind of rain that leaves a residue on your skin if you stand in it too long. The street is a narrow canyon between tower blocks, the sky reduced to a sliver of grey clogged with drone traffic and hanging cables.

The noise is immediate and overwhelming. Vendors shouting prices in three languages. The thrum of generators. Music bleeding from a dozen different sources, competing for dominance in a war nobody wins. A street preacher stands on an overturned crate, screaming about the sin of stack transfers, about souls being split and copied until nothing human remains.

People flow around you in a current of damp clothes and desperate purpose. Augmented laborers with hydraulic arms heading to the freight docks. A group of elven refugees huddled under a tarp, their belongings in plastic bags. Two orc bouncers flanking the entrance to an underground fighting pit, their arms crossed like castle gates.

text.text: The bouncers clock you immediately and give you the nod, a subtle chin-lift that means 'you're one of us.' Down here, orc solidarity isn't sentimentality. It's survival infrastructure.

The elven refugees look up as you pass. One of them reaches out and murmurs something in Old Elvish, a blessing for travelers. Their eyes hold the particular despair of a people who remember forests and clean water.

A baseline human in the Lower District is invisible in the best possible way. No one looks at you with suspicion or pity. You're just another body in the current, unremarkable, unnoticed. In a place like this, anonymity is armor.

choices.text: Browse the vendor stalls
Head toward Mako's shop
Listen to the street preacher

node_007.title: The Vendor Stalls
text: The stalls line the street under sagging tarps and corrugated metal awnings. Everything down here is secondhand, stolen, or counterfeit. A woman with mechanical eyes sells pre-loaded data chips from a folding table, her gaze tracking every hand that comes near her merchandise. Next to her, an old man offers military-surplus ration packs and water purification tablets, the currency of the truly desperate.

Further down, a vendor with a shaved head and a neck full of tattoos deals in black-market augmentations. Fingers, eyes, neural interfaces, all laid out on synthetic velvet like grotesque jewelry. A hand-painted sign reads: INSTALLATION AVAILABLE. NO ANESTHETIC. DISCOUNT FOR CASH.
You're examining a display of signal scramblers when you feel a tug at your jacket. Light fingers, quick and practiced, reaching for your credit chips.
A kid, no older than twelve, hair matted, eyes too sharp for that young a face.

choices.text: [Dexterity 12] Grab the kid's wrist
Let it go. The kid needs it more than you.

node_007a.title: Quick Hands
text: Your hand snaps down and catches the kid's wrist. The child freezes, eyes going wide, then immediately starts struggling like a trapped animal. The credit chips tumble from their fingers and you catch them with your other hand.
"Let go," the kid hisses. "Let go or I'll scream and tell everyone you touched me."
Up close, you can see the grime ground into their skin, the hollows under their eyes. This isn't a game for them. This is how they eat.

text.text: The kid stares up at your face and the bravado falters. Not from your appearance but from something they see in your eyes. Recognition, maybe. The shared knowledge of what it means to be discarded.

choices.text: Give the kid a credit chip and tell them to find honest work
Take your chips back and walk away
Ask the kid what they know about the neighborhood

node_007b.title: Too Slow
text: Your hand closes on empty air. The kid is already three stalls away, weaving through the crowd with the fluid grace of someone who learned to run before they learned to walk. Your credit chips go with them. Not all of them, but enough to sting.

The vendor with the mechanical eyes watched the whole thing without blinking. "First time in Sector 7?" she asks, her voice flat with amusement. "Keep your hands in your pockets. Keep your money closer. And don't chase them. The pack watches out for each other. You'll get swarmed."
Lesson learned. The Lower District takes what it can, when it can, from whoever it can.

choices.text: Take the vendor's advice. Move on toward Mako's.
Listen to the street preacher nearby

node_007c.title: Mercy in the Slums
text: You let the kid take the chips. They vanish into the crowd like smoke, clutching their prize with both hands. You watch them disappear into an alley where two more children are waiting, and then all three are gone.
It costs you, but you remember what it was like to be that desperate. Or maybe you just don't have the energy to care about a few credits when the whole system is designed to drain you dry anyway.

The vendor with mechanical eyes watches you and something shifts in her expression. Not pity. Respect, maybe, or at least the absence of contempt. "You're soft for this district," she says. "That'll either get you killed or get you friends. Hard to say which is worse."

choices.text: Head toward Mako's shop
Listen to the street preacher

node_007d.title: A Small Kindness
text: You press a credit chip into the kid's palm and release their wrist. They stare at the chip, then at you, suspicion warring with something softer. "Honest work," the kid repeats, as if the words are in a foreign language. Then they grin, a quick flash of teeth. "Sure. Honest." And they're gone.

You won't see that credit chip again, and the kid won't be finding honest work. But the exchange bought you something you can't quantify. Down here, people notice kindness. It's rare enough to be memorable.
Somewhere in the crowd, someone saw what you did. You can feel their gaze, steady and appraising, even after you turn away.

choices.text: Continue to Mako's shop

node_007e.title: Street Intelligence
text: The kid stops struggling and looks at you with an expression far too calculating for someone their age. "Information costs," they say. "Even down here."

You show them a credit chip. They snatch it and it vanishes into their clothing. "Depends what you want to know. The block crews are fighting over the east corridor. Iron Collective recruiters have been coming through, looking for warm bodies. Some guy in a black coat has been asking about someone matching your description. And Mako's been busy, lots of people coming and going from her shop at weird hours."
The kid pulls their wrist free while you're processing that. "The guy in the black coat. He smelled like the upper levels. Clean. You know? Nobody down here smells clean."
Then they're gone.

choices.text: A man in a black coat asking about you. Get to Mako's. Now.
Listen to the street preacher first

node_008.title: The Preacher
text: The street preacher stands on a plastic crate that bows under his weight. He's gaunt, wild-eyed, with a voice that cuts through the street noise like a blade. His robes are stained and patched, but the symbol stitched on his chest is unmistakable: the broken circle of the Ashen Circle, the anti-stack movement.

"They tell you it's immortality!" he screams, jabbing a finger at the Meridian advertisement overhead. "They tell you your soul transfers with your memories! But I have seen the truth! I have seen what comes out the other side of a stack transfer and it is NOT the person who went in! It wears their face! It speaks with their voice! But the light behind the eyes is GONE!"
A small crowd has gathered. Some nod along. Others watch with the blank patience of people who have heard this speech before. A patrol drone hovers at the edge of the crowd, recording.

The preacher sees you watching and locks eyes. "You. I can see it on you. The weight. Someone wants to put a stack in your neck, don't they? Someone wants to own your continuity. Let me tell you what the corporations won't: once they copy you, the original becomes disposable. You become a rough draft of yourself."

text.text: The preacher's eyes widen when he sees you clearly. A synth. You are, in a sense, the very thing he rails against, a consciousness in an artificial body. But something in his expression isn't hatred. It's pity. "You already know," he whispers, briefly breaking his performance. "You know what it's like to wonder if you're the original."

choices.text: Ask the preacher about the Ashen Circle
Move on. You've heard enough sermons.

node_008a.title: The Ashen Circle
text: The preacher steps down from his crate and grips your arm with surprising strength. His eyes are bloodshot but focused. "The Ashen Circle isn't a religion," he says, voice dropping to a fierce whisper. "It's a reckoning. The stacks are killing what it means to be mortal. What it means to be real. The corporations have turned death into a product and life into a subscription service."

He presses a small metal token into your palm. An ash-grey circle, broken at the top. "Keep this. When you're ready to understand what they're really building up in those towers, when you want to know why people are disappearing from the Lower District, find us. Back alley behind the water reclamation plant, sunset service. Ask for Brother Callum."
The patrol drone drifts closer. The preacher releases your arm and climbs back onto his crate, resuming his public sermon at full volume as if the private conversation never happened.

choices.text: Pocket the token and head to Mako's

node_009.title: Approaching Mako's
text: Three blocks east. You navigate the street by memory and instinct, cutting through the crowd, stepping over a man sleeping in a puddle of rainwater, skirting the perimeter of a dice game where the stakes look like they've escalated past friendly.

Mako's Repair Shop is wedged into the ground floor of a residential block like a tumor the building has learned to live with. The storefront is a chaos of hanging cables, gutted electronics, and hand-painted signs advertising services in four languages. A neon sign in the window buzzes: MAKO'S, FIX ANYTHING, ASK NOTHING.

Through the grease-smeared window, you can see Mako herself at her workbench, hunched over what looks like a disassembled neural interface. She's a compact woman of indeterminate age, her arms sleeved in functional augmentations that she built and installed herself. Her hair is cropped short and streaked with chemical burns. She looks up as you approach the door, and her expression tells you she's been expecting you.

choices.text: Enter the shop
Check the alley beside the shop first

node_010.title: Mako's Repair Shop
text: The door chime is a salvaged drone speaker that plays a distorted three-note tone when you enter. The shop smells of solder, machine oil, and the ozone tang of active electronics. Every surface is covered in parts, tools, and half-finished projects. A medical-grade prosthetic hand sits in a vice, its fingers twitching in a test pattern. Shelves sag under the weight of salvaged components sorted into jars by type and size.
Mako sets down her tools and fixes you with a look that's equal parts assessment and warning. "Close the door," she says. "Lock it. The magnetic lock, not the bolt."
You do as she asks. The magnetic lock engages with a heavy thunk, and you notice the shop's windows dim slightly. Privacy glass. This conversation isn't for the street.

"Someone's looking for you," Mako says without preamble. "Not street-level looking. Tower-level. The kind of looking that comes with resources and patience and a very specific list of names. Your name is on that list."

choices.text: "Who's looking for me?"
"How do you know my name?"
"Was it you who sent the anonymous message?"

node_011.title: The Search
text: Mako pulls a battered datapad from under her workbench and slides it across to you. The screen shows a blurry surveillance image of a man in a dark coat standing at the entrance to your building. The timestamp is from last night. His face is obscured by a privacy mask, the kind that scrambles facial recognition software, but his posture radiates authority. Corporate authority.

"I don't know who he is," Mako says. "But I know what he represents. That coat is Meridian executive-tier. The mask is Neon Court tech. And the signal traffic coming off him when he was near my shop nearly fried my monitoring equipment. He's carrying military-grade encryption hardware."

She taps the screen. "He was here two hours ago. Asked about you by description, not by name. Said he was offering an opportunity. People in this district know I hear things, so they pointed him here. I told him nothing."

choices.text: [Wisdom 13] Read Mako's body language. Is she telling the truth?
"What does he want with me?"

node_012.title: How She Knows
text: Mako almost smiles. Almost. "I know everyone in Sector 7. It's my business to know. When you moved into that box on the seventh floor three months ago, I ran your face through every database I could access. Most came back blank, which is suspicious by itself. Clean records in the Lower District means someone scrubbed them. And scrubbing takes money and connections."

She leans back in her chair and crosses her augmented arms. The servos in her forearms whir quietly. "You're either running from something or hiding from something. Either way, someone with resources put you here and went to a lot of trouble to make you invisible. And now, someone else with resources is trying to make you visible again."

text.text: "Especially odd for a synth," she adds, eyeing you. "Your registration says domestic service model, but your chassis is custom work. Combat-rated alloys, military-spec joints. Someone built you for more than washing dishes, and then filed you under the wrong category. Why?"

"Your augments are interesting, too," she adds, gesturing at your prosthetics. "The external casings are low-end but the internal architecture is top-shelf. Someone gave you quality hardware and then dressed it down to look cheap. That takes effort. That's deliberate camouflage."

choices.text: "Who's looking for me, then?"
"What do you want from me, Mako?"

node_013.title: The Sender
text: Mako shakes her head. "Not me. I would have just sent one of the kids to bang on your door. The message came from someone in the Ghost Network, a contact I've been working with for the past year. They don't use names. They don't meet face to face. They communicate in dead drops, encrypted bursts, and self-deleting messages."

She reaches under her workbench and produces a small data chip, matte black, no markings. "They left this for you. Told me to give it to you when you came in, and to tell you not to plug it into anything connected to the district network. Air-gapped devices only."

The chip sits on the workbench between you, small and heavy with implication. The Ghost Network. You've heard the name whispered in the corridors and food lines. A decentralized resistance movement, or a conspiracy theory, or a corporate honeypot, depending on who you ask.

choices.text: Take the chip
"I don't trust gifts from people who won't show their face."

node_013a.title: The Data Chip
text: You pocket the chip. It's warm, the faintest vibration suggesting active circuitry inside. Whatever data it holds, it's waiting to be read.

Mako nods, satisfied. "The Network doesn't reach out to just anyone. Whatever they see in you, it's enough to make them break their own protocols. They're careful people. Paranoid, even. For them to initiate contact means they think you're worth the risk."
She pauses, choosing her next words with the precision of someone calibrating a delicate instrument. "Or it means they think the risk of not contacting you is worse."

choices.text: "What do you know about what's on this chip?"
"Who's looking for me? What can you tell me?"

node_014.title: Reading Mako
text: You watch Mako as she talks. The micro-expressions, the timing of her blinks, the way her augmented fingers fidget with a solder pen. Your instincts read her like a schematic.

She's telling the truth. Mostly. She genuinely doesn't know who the man in the black coat is. But she knows more than she's sharing about why he's looking for you. There's a calculation happening behind her eyes, a cost-benefit analysis of how much to reveal. She's protecting someone. Maybe herself. Maybe you. Maybe someone else entirely.
She also glances at a spot beneath her workbench twice during the conversation. There's something there she's considering showing you. She hasn't decided yet.

choices.text: [Charisma 14] "Mako, I can see you're holding back. I need to know everything."
"What's under the workbench, Mako?"

node_014a.title: Can't Read Her
text: You try to get a read on Mako, but she's been in the information business too long. Her face gives you nothing. Every expression could be genuine or performance and you can't tell the difference. Some people wear masks made of metal and polymer. Mako's mask is made of years of practice.

choices.text: "What does the man want with me?"

node_014b.title: What Mako Knows
text: Mako stares at you for a long moment. Then she reaches under the workbench and pulls out a sealed document tube, the old-fashioned kind, analog, no digital footprint. She rolls it across the table to you.

"Three weeks ago, a Ghost Network contact gave me this and told me to hold onto it until you came asking questions. I didn't open it. I don't want to know what's inside. But they said it's related to a project called LATENCY, something Meridian Corp buried deep in their classified research division."

She looks at the tube, then at you. "They also said that the people looking for you aren't just corporate recruiters. They're a retrieval team. You're not being offered an opportunity. You're being hunted. And whatever's in that tube will tell you why."

The word 'LATENCY' means nothing to you. But somewhere in the back of your mind, beneath the fog of daily survival, something flickers. Not a memory. More like the shadow of one, the shape of a room you've been in but can't remember entering.

choices.text: "LATENCY. What is it?"
Take the tube and leave. You need to find somewhere safe to open it.

node_015.title: Mako's Warning
text: Mako pushes back from the workbench and stands. She's shorter than you expected, but the way she carries herself makes the room feel smaller. "I've told you what I can. What I will. I'm a mechanic and a broker, not a bodyguard. But I'll give you this for free: the Lower District has a job board at the intersection of Block 4 and the recycling corridor. Posted work, no questions asked. You need credits and you need connections, and that board provides both."

She moves to the door and disengages the magnetic lock. "The man in the black coat was heading west when he left here. If you go east toward the job board, you'll put distance between you and whatever he represents. At least for now."
She opens the door. Rain and noise flood in. "Come back when you need repairs. Or information. Both cost credits, but I give a neighborhood discount."
The dismissal is clear. Whatever else Mako knows, she's decided you've heard enough for one visit.

choices.text: Head toward the job board
Go west. Find the man in the black coat.

node_015a.title: Leaving Mako's
text: You tuck the document tube inside your jacket. It's heavier than it should be, or maybe that's just the weight of not knowing what's inside. Mako watches you go with an expression that might be concern, or might just be the look of someone calculating whether they've bet on the right horse.
"Job board," she says as you reach the door. "Block 4 and the recycling corridor. You need resources before you open that tube. Trust me on this."

The door closes behind you and you're back in the rain. The street feels different now, charged with the knowledge that someone from the towers is down here looking for you. Every shadow could be a threat. Every stranger could be a spotter.

choices.text: Head toward the job board
Duck into the alley to collect your thoughts

node_016.title: The Alley
text: The alley between Mako's shop and the adjacent building is narrow and dark, the kind of space where the rain doesn't quite reach and neither does the law. Dumpsters overflow with electronic waste. A rat the size of a small cat scurries over a pile of discarded circuit boards, its eyes reflecting green in the dim light.
You're halfway through the alley when you hear footsteps behind you. Heavy. Deliberate. More than one person.
"Hey." The voice is flat and bored, the practiced tone of someone who does this often enough that the threat is routine. "Nice jacket. Hand it over. And whatever's in your pockets."

You turn. Three of them. Street thugs, augmented, scarred, built for violence. The one who spoke has a metal jaw and a shock baton hanging loose in his right hand. The other two flank him, one with a blade, one with bare fists wrapped in steel-fiber knuckle guards.

text.text: Metal-jaw reassesses you as you turn. His confidence wavers when he sees your full frame, the tusks, the shoulders that barely fit the alley. "Big one," he mutters to his crew. "Doesn't matter. Three on one." But his grip shifts on the baton.

Metal-jaw's eyes drop to your augmented limbs, reading the hardware. You see him calculating: are your prosthetics combat-rated, or are they the cheap civilian models that can barely grip a door handle? He can't tell from the exterior casing. That uncertainty is a weapon in itself.

choices.text: [Fight] Draw your knife and stand your ground
[Charisma 14] Talk your way out of this
[Dexterity 13] Run for the other end of the alley
[Strength 15] Crack your knuckles and step toward them. "You sure about this?"

node_017.title: Alley Fight
text: You pull the knife and drop into a stance. The electrical tape on the handle bites into your palm. Metal-jaw grins, and the grin is worse than the baton. "Hard way it is."
He swings first. The baton crackles blue-white as it arcs toward your head.

node_017a.title: Intimidation
text: You step forward. Not fast, not aggressive. Slow. Deliberate. You let them see the size of you, the way you fill the alley, the absolute absence of fear in your expression. You crack your knuckles and the sound echoes off the wet walls like breaking bone.

"Three of you," you say, and your voice is the calm that comes before the kind of storm that doesn't leave survivors. "Three of you against me. In a space this narrow. Where your numbers don't mean a thing."
Metal-jaw's baton hand drops half an inch. The one with the blade takes a step back without realizing it. The brawler looks at his companions, waiting for someone else to move first.
"Walk away," you say. "Walk away and you get to keep your teeth. All of them. Which is more than the last three who tried this have."
Metal-jaw stares at you for a long three seconds. Then he spits on the ground, turns, and walks. His crew follows. No words. No face-saving threats. Just the wet sound of retreating footsteps.
You breathe.

choices.text: Continue through the alley toward the job board

node_017b.title: Victory in the Alley
text: The last thug hits the ground and doesn't get up. The alley is quiet except for the rain and the groaning of the beaten. Metal-jaw lies against the dumpster, his shock baton sparking uselessly in a puddle. The other two are scattered like broken furniture.

Your knife hand is shaking, but not from fear. Adrenaline. The fight was dirty, desperate, and real. Nothing like the choreographed violence on the entertainment feeds. Down here, combat is ugly and it hurts and the line between winning and dying is measured in inches.

You search the bodies. Metal-jaw has a few credit chips and a comm device with encrypted contacts. The blade thug carries a decent knife, better than yours. The brawler has nothing but bruises and regret.

choices.text: Take the better knife and move on
Check Metal-jaw's comm device

node_017c.title: Beaten Down
text: The baton catches you across the ribs and the world goes white, then grey, then very far away. You hit the ground. Boots. Pain. The sound of your pockets being turned inside out. Credit chips scatter on wet concrete and are snatched up by fast hands.

When you can see again, they're gone. So are your credits and most of your pride. But they left you your knife, either as an insult or an oversight, and they left you alive, which in the Lower District passes for mercy.
You pull yourself up against the dumpster and take stock. Bruised ribs, split lip, empty pockets. But you're breathing and you're angry, and down here, anger is a renewable resource.

choices.text: Limp toward the job board. You need credits more than ever now.

node_017d.title: The Comm Device
text: Metal-jaw's comm device is a ruggedized model, waterproof, shockproof, the kind of hardware street-level thugs don't usually carry. You scroll through the contacts. Most are listed under aliases: Switchblade, Crux, Alley Cat. But one entry stands out.
The contact is labeled simply "IC-R" and the last message reads: SECTOR 7 RECRUITMENT ONGOING. CANDIDATES NEEDED FOR DOCK OPERATION. PRIORITY: COMBAT-CAPABLE. BONUS FOR AUGMENTED.
IC-R. Iron Collective Recruiter. These thugs weren't just mugging people. They were vetting them. The ones who fought back got noticed. The ones who won got recruited.
Which means someone might be very interested that you just beat their talent scouts unconscious.

choices.text: Pocket the comm. Could be useful. Head to the job board.

node_018.title: Talking It Down
text: You hold up your hands, palms out, showing empty fingers. "Let me save you some time," you say, keeping your voice even and conversational. "I've got maybe fifteen credits to my name and a knife held together with tape. You hit me, I go down, you split fifteen credits three ways. That's five each. Five credits. You can't buy a hot meal for five credits."
Metal-jaw pauses. The math is working on him.
"Or," you continue, "you let me walk, and I owe you a favor. I'm headed to the job board. Whatever work I pick up, you get a cut of my first payout. That's going to be a lot more than five credits."
The thugs exchange looks. Metal-jaw scratches his chrome jaw with the end of his baton, thinking it over. "First payout. Twenty percent."
"Ten."
"Fifteen, and I don't break your kneecaps as a signing bonus."
"Fifteen. Deal."
Metal-jaw nods and steps aside. "We'll find you," he says as you pass. "We always find the people who owe us."

choices.text: Head to the job board. You've got a debt to cover now.

node_018a.title: Words Aren't Enough
text: You try to talk, but Metal-jaw isn't buying. "Shut up," he says, stepping forward. "I've heard every line in the book. You're not special. You're just another mark."
The baton rises. Words have failed. You've got seconds to decide.

choices.text: [Fight] Draw your knife
[Dexterity 13] Run for it

node_019.title: Clean Getaway
text: You bolt. Not toward them and not straight back, but sideways, vaulting a dumpster and hitting a fire escape ladder that groans but holds. You're up one flight before they react, two flights before they reach the ladder, and by the third flight you've kicked the ladder loose and it crashes down behind you in a shriek of rusted metal.

Cursing echoes up from the alley below, but none of them can climb the smooth wall. You pull yourself onto a rooftop access walkway and keep moving, navigating the elevated maze of catwalks and bridges that connect the buildings at mid-level.

The rain hits harder up here, but the air is cleaner and you can see the job board's neon glow two blocks to the east. From above, the Lower District looks like a circuit board, lines of light tracing paths between dark nodes. You descend a maintenance ladder on the far side and drop back to street level.

choices.text: Make your way to the job board

node_019a.title: Caught Running
text: You run but your foot catches on a cable snaking across the alley floor. You stumble, catch yourself, but lose the precious seconds you needed. The brawler is fast for his size and his fist catches you in the kidney as you try to round the corner.

You hit the wall, bounce off, and keep moving on pure survival instinct. They chase you for half a block before giving up, but the brawler got a good hit in and your side is screaming. They also got your credit chips, snatched from your pocket in the collision.

You lean against a wall three blocks away, breathing hard, feeling the bruise bloom across your lower back. Lost some credits and some dignity, but kept your teeth. In the Lower District, call that a draw.

choices.text: Find the job board. You need credits badly now.

node_020.title: The Job Board
text: The intersection of Block 4 and the recycling corridor is the closest thing Sector 7 has to a town square. A covered area where four streets meet, sheltered from the rain by overlapping awnings and the bulk of the recycling plant above. At its center stands the job board.

It's a repurposed industrial display screen, eight feet tall, bolted to a concrete pillar. Jobs scroll past in colored text: red for dangerous, yellow for semi-legal, green for legitimate work, which means the green entries are rare and pay poorly. Each listing has a contact code and a brief description. No names. No interviews. You take the code, you do the job, you get paid. Simple as the economy of desperation.
A crowd mills around the board, studying the listings with the intensity of people for whom the next meal depends on the next job. You scan the current postings.

text.text: Your optical zoom lets you read the board from the edge of the crowd while everyone else pushes forward, squinting. Several listings specifically request augmented workers, offering better rates for enhanced capabilities. The Lower District may despise what you are, but it'll pay extra for what you can do.

A few people in the crowd edge away as you approach the board. More room for you. Some listings have a small glyph next to the contact code, a circle with a line through it: no orcs. It's illegal to discriminate, but down here the law is more of a suggestion.

choices.text: Check the enforcer listings (red)
Check the hacker listings (yellow)
Check the medic listings (green)
Check the smuggler listings (yellow)
Ignore the board. A man in a long coat is watching you from across the square.

node_021.title: Enforcer Work
text: The red listings pulse with urgency. Most are straightforward: guard duty at a warehouse, debt collection for a loan shark, standing muscle at an underground fighting ring. The pay is decent but the mortality rate hovers between 'concerning' and 'statistical certainty.'

One listing catches your eye. SECURITY ESCORT NEEDED. DOCK DISTRICT. PACKAGE DELIVERY. NO QUESTIONS. 200 CREDITS. The contact code is a string of numbers followed by the letters IC, Iron Collective. Mako mentioned them. They're one of the major factions in the lower city, a worker's militia that started as a labor union and evolved into something between a private army and a political movement.
Two hundred credits would cover food and rent for two weeks. It would also put you on the Iron Collective's radar, for better or worse.

choices.text: Take the enforcer job code
Check other listings instead

node_022.title: Hacker Work
text: The yellow listings are the grey market: not legal, not violent, somewhere in the profitable middle. The hacking jobs require skill and nerve. DATA EXTRACTION FROM CORPORATE TERMINAL. SIGNAL INTERCEPTION AND DECRYPTION. DRONE NETWORK INTRUSION, TEMPORARY ACCESS REQUIRED.

One posting stands out from the rest, cleaner text, better formatting, as if whoever wrote it was used to a higher standard of communication. INFORMATION SPECIALIST NEEDED. ACQUIRE PERSONNEL RECORDS FROM MID-LEVEL CLINIC. RECORDS TO BE DELIVERED ENCRYPTED, DEAD DROP. 175 CREDITS. BONUS FOR CLEAN EXTRACTION (NO TRACES). The contact code has no faction marker, but the precision of the language suggests Ghost Network.

text.text: Your architecture gives you a natural advantage in digital infiltration. Your wireless protocols can interface directly with most commercial systems. The listing might as well have your name on it.

choices.text: Take the hacker job code
Check other listings instead

node_023.title: Medic Work
text: The green listings are sparse and the pay reflects the district's opinion of legitimate work: low. But the medic postings have a different quality to them. In a place where augmentation surgery is done without anesthetic and street fights are a nightly occurrence, medical skills are currency.

FIELD MEDIC NEEDED FOR COMMUNITY CLINIC. FLEXIBLE HOURS. 100 CREDITS/WEEK. SUPPLIES PROVIDED. The contact code carries a small cross symbol, marking it as Ashen Circle affiliated. They run the only free clinic in Sector 7, serving the people who can't afford corporate healthcare, which is nearly everyone.

Another listing: COMBAT MEDIC. ACCOMPANY RETRIEVAL TEAM. PATCH AND GO. 150 CREDITS PER RUN. HIGH RISK. NO FACTION MARKER. This one is unsigned, the kind of job where you don't know who you're working for until you show up.

text.text: Elven physiology carries an intuitive understanding of organic systems that no amount of human medical training can replicate. Your hands were shaped by evolution for precise, delicate work. In the age of augmentation, that makes you an anachronism. In a clinic that treats flesh and blood, it makes you invaluable.

choices.text: Take the clinic medic job code
Take the combat medic job code
Check other listings instead

node_024.title: Smuggler Work
text: The smuggling listings are coded more carefully than the others. Half the text is euphemism. 'Package relocation.' 'Inventory redistribution.' 'Alternative supply chain management.' Everyone knows what they mean. The patrol drones know too, but the listings use enough plausible deniability to stay on the board.

COURIER NEEDED. LOWER TO MID-LEVEL TRANSIT. SMALL PACKAGE. CHECKPOINT AVOIDANCE REQUIRED. 180 CREDITS. DISCRETION ESSENTIAL. The contact code bears a subtle watermark visible only at certain angles: the Neon Court, the faction that controls the entertainment district and most of the illicit trade between the city's vertical layers.

The Neon Court deals in everything the towers want but can't admit to wanting: narcotics, unlicensed stacks, black-market augmentations, forbidden entertainment. Working for them pays well and costs your soul in installments.

choices.text: Take the smuggler job code
Check other listings instead

node_025.title: Job Accepted
text: You memorize the contact code and step back from the board. The job is logged in your datapad, a string of numbers that represents your next meal, your rent, your survival. In the Lower District, a job code is more reliable than a promise and more dangerous than a loaded weapon.

The crowd shifts around you as other job seekers press forward, and you become aware of someone watching you. Not the casual surveillance of the patrol drones or the territorial assessment of other job seekers. This is focused. Intentional.
A man stands at the edge of the square, half-hidden by the shadow of a support pillar. Long coat. Dark. Clean. The kind of clean that doesn't exist below the mid-levels.
He's looking directly at you.

choices.text: Approach the man in the coat
Pretend you didn't notice. Walk the other way.

node_026.title: The Stack Dealer
text: The man steps out of the shadow as you approach, and up close he's exactly what the kid described: clean. His skin has the healthy glow of someone who eats real food and breathes filtered air. His coat is a material you don't have a name for, something that repels the rain without getting wet. His eyes are augmented, the irises shifting through spectrums of blue as they focus on you.

"Remarkable," he says, and his voice is modulated, smooth, pitched to inspire trust. Corporate vocal tuning. "I've been watching you since you left your building. The way you move through this district. You don't belong here. You know it. I know it. The question is whether you're ready to do something about it."

He reaches into his coat and produces a small case, matte black, hinged. He opens it. Inside, nestled in foam, is a memory stack. It catches the neon light and scatters it into rainbows. It's beautiful. It's also worth more than everything in this district combined.

"Meridian executive-grade," he says. "Full consciousness transfer capability. Self-sustaining power cell. Encrypted personality matrix with room for a lifetime of memories. Retail value: more than you'll earn in ten years. I'm offering it to you for a fraction of that. Call it an investment in your potential."

choices.text: "What's the catch?"
"I can't afford a fraction of ten years' earnings either."
[Wisdom 14] Study the stack carefully before responding
[Tech 13] Examine the stack's hardware specifications

node_027.title: The Offer
text: The man smiles. It doesn't reach his augmented eyes. "The arrangement is simple. I provide the stack. You provide a service, at a time and place of my employer's choosing. One job. After that, the stack is yours, fully registered, fully legal. You go from being a Lower District ghost to being someone with a future. With continuity. With the possibility of tomorrow."

He lets the word hang in the air. Tomorrow. In the Lower District, tomorrow is a luxury. Most people down here live in a perpetual present tense, planning no further than the next meal, the next rent payment. A stack changes that calculation entirely. A stack means even if your body fails, you continue. You persist.

"My employer values talent," the man continues. "And talent is wasted down here. Think of this as a recruitment offer. No commitment beyond the single job. If you don't like what you see, walk away after. Keep the stack. No strings."
Nobody in the Lower District believes in 'no strings.' But the stack gleams in its case like a second chance made solid.

choices.text: Accept the stack. One job for a future is a deal you can't refuse.
Refuse. Nothing is free, especially not from people this clean.
[Wisdom 14] Study the stack more carefully

node_028.title: Something Wrong
text: You look past the polish and the pitch. Something about the man's presentation is too smooth, too rehearsed. He's delivered this speech before, probably to other people in other slum districts, and the script hasn't changed because it doesn't need to. It works on the desperate. It's designed for the desperate.

But there's more. You notice the way he positioned himself with the support pillar behind him, blocking the patrol drone's line of sight. His coat has a subtle electromagnetic shimmer, an active privacy field. Whatever this transaction is, he doesn't want it recorded.

And the stack itself. It's beautiful. But there's a hairline seam along the casing that doesn't match Meridian's manufacturing standards. This isn't a factory unit. It's been opened. Modified. Someone has been inside the hardware.

choices.text: "This stack has been tampered with. What's really on it?"
Walk away. This deal stinks.

node_029.title: Technical Analysis
text: You lean in and examine the stack with a practiced eye. The external casing is Meridian exec-grade, that much is genuine. But the internal architecture tells a different story. The power cell is aftermarket, higher capacity than stock, the kind used in military prototypes. The data interface ports have been re-soldered with non-standard connectors.

Most tellingly, there's a secondary chipset piggy-backed onto the main consciousness transfer module. It's small, nearly invisible, integrated with surgical precision. You've seen this kind of modification before. It's a tracker. Whoever installs this stack doesn't just get immortality. They get permanent surveillance. Every thought backed up to an external server. Every memory accessible by whoever controls the relay.
This isn't a gift. It's a leash.

choices.text: Confront the dealer about the tracking chip
Say nothing. Refuse politely. Leave.

node_029a.title: Confrontation
text: The man's expression doesn't change when you call out the modification, which tells you everything his face won't. He expected some targets to notice. He has a contingency for this.

"Perceptive," he says, closing the case with a snap. "My employer said you might be. That's part of why you're on the list." The warmth drains from his voice like heat from a corpse. What's left is flat and professional. "The offer stands. Modified stack, full surveillance capability. The alternative is that we acquire the data we need through less voluntary means. Your file suggests you have information relevant to a project of significant corporate interest."
He tucks the case back into his coat. "You have forty-eight hours. After that, the acquisition team arrives, and they don't make offers. They make extractions."

He turns and walks into the crowd. The rain swallows him within seconds, and you're left standing in the square with a deadline and a threat and the unshakable feeling that your life just became significantly more complicated.

choices.text: You need allies. Fast. Look around the square.

node_030.title: Stack Accepted
text: You take the case. The stack sits in your hand, lighter than it should be, as if the promise of immortality has no physical weight. The man smiles and produces a card, matte grey, with a single comm frequency printed on one side.

"When you're ready for installation, contact this frequency. We have facilities. Clean, professional, painless. And when the job comes, you'll receive instructions through the stack's secure channel."
He extends his hand. You shake it. His grip is firm, dry, practiced. "Welcome to the future," he says. "Try not to waste it."

He vanishes into the crowd with the efficiency of someone accustomed to disappearing. You're left holding a case worth more than your life and the unmistakable sense that you've just signed a contract you haven't read.

text.text: A stack in a synthetic body. The irony is sharp enough to cut. You're already a transferred consciousness. Adding a stack would make you a copy of a copy, a reflection of a reflection. The philosophical implications would be paralyzing if you had time for philosophy.

choices.text: Pocket the stack and look around the square

node_030a.title: Stack Refused
text: "No," you say. The word is simple and final.
The man's smile thins. The augmented eyes cycle through three shades of blue in rapid succession, a processing tell he probably doesn't know he has. He's not used to being refused.

"You're making a mistake," he says, and the smooth corporate affect slips for just a moment, revealing something colder underneath. "The offer won't come again. And when the people I work for decide they need something from this district, they don't ask twice. They take."
He closes the case and tucks it back into his coat. "Forty-eight hours," he says. "After that, a different team arrives. They're less conversational than I am."
He walks away. The crowd closes behind him like water filling a void. You've refused a king's ransom and earned a deadline. In the Lower District, that's called a Tuesday.

choices.text: You need allies. Look around the square.

node_030b.title: Uneasy Decision
text: You study the stack, looking for anything wrong, but the craftsmanship is beyond your ability to evaluate. It could be legitimate. It could be a trap. The uncertainty gnaws at you, but the opportunity is real and the desperation is realer.
The man watches you deliberate with patient amusement. "Take your time," he says. "But not too much time. I have other appointments."

choices.text: Accept the stack. The risk is worth the reward.
Refuse. Your gut says walk away.

node_031.title: The Square
text: The square is busy with the controlled chaos of Lower District commerce. You scan the crowd, taking stock. Something has shifted in your awareness. The anonymous message, Mako's warning, the man in the coat. You're no longer just surviving. You're being drawn into something larger, and the forces pulling at you have names.
Three things catch your attention simultaneously.

Near the recycling plant entrance, a woman in heavy work gear stands on a crate, distributing pamphlets. She's muscular, confident, with the bearing of someone used to giving orders. The pamphlets bear the clenched-fist emblem of the Iron Collective.

On the wall of a building opposite, a massive holographic advertisement flickers to life. It's a Neon Court production, slick and seductive: a paradise of lights and pleasure and escape. COME TO THE NEON COURT, the voice purrs. FORGET YOUR TROUBLES. FORGET YOURSELF.

And in a shadowed doorway, barely visible, a small drone hovers at knee height. It's not a patrol drone. It's too small, too quiet, too deliberate. A data chip is taped to its underside, and a tiny light blinks green when it detects your presence.

choices.text: Approach the Iron Collective recruiter
Watch the Neon Court advertisement
Investigate the small drone

node_032.title: Iron Collective
text: The recruiter sees you coming and straightens on her crate, sizing you up with the efficiency of someone who's spent years evaluating potential assets. Her name tag reads SGT. VASQUEZ, and the scars on her hands say her rank wasn't earned behind a desk.
"You look like you can handle yourself," she says without preamble. "Can you?"

She doesn't wait for an answer. "The Iron Collective isn't a gang. We're not a charity. We're a workers' coalition with the muscle to back up our demands. The corporations strip this district for labor and leave us the scraps. We're done with scraps. We're organizing, and we need people who can fight, who can think, and who aren't afraid of the drones."

She hands you a pamphlet. It's dense with text: workers' rights, equitable stack access, an end to corporate extraction of Lower District resources. At the bottom, an address and a time. "We meet every three days. Come to one meeting. Listen. If you don't like what you hear, walk out. No pressure. No obligation."

text.text: Vasquez gives you an extra look. "We have a strong orc contingent," she says. "Commander Gresh leads our Sector 9 operations. She doesn't tolerate the discrimination the rest of this city runs on. You'd have a place with us. A real one."

"Baseline human," she notes, not unkindly. "Good. We need people who can walk through a scanner without setting off alarms. Half our operations require someone the drones won't flag for enhanced screening."

choices.text: "I'll be there."
"I'll think about it."
Take the pamphlet but check out the other factions too

node_033.title: Iron Collective Contact
text: Vasquez nods. Whether you committed or hedged, she's heard both responses a thousand times and values neither until action backs them up. "Actions speak," she says. "Show up or don't. But if you do, bring your spine. We're not a social club."

She pulls a small metal pin from her pocket and presses it into your hand. It's an iron circle, the Collective's emblem. "Show this at the door. It'll get you in. Lose it and you'll need to find another way."

She's already turning to the next potential recruit before you've pocketed the pin. The Iron Collective doesn't waste time on sentimentality. They run like the factories they aim to liberate: efficient, relentless, productive.

choices.text: Check out the Neon Court advertisement
Investigate the small drone in the doorway
You've seen enough. Time to wrap up and plan your next move.

node_034.title: Neon Court Advertisement
text: The holographic advertisement is a masterpiece of sensory manipulation. It projects a world of color and warmth onto the grey concrete, turning the rain-soaked wall into a window overlooking paradise. Beautiful people in beautiful clothes moving through spaces designed for pleasure. Music that bypasses your ears and speaks directly to the part of your brain that craves escape.

NEON COURT. WHERE THE NIGHT NEVER ENDS. The voice is gender-neutral, warm, intimate, as if it's speaking only to you. WHERE EVERYONE IS WELCOME. WHERE EVERY DESIRE IS POSSIBLE. VISIT OUR ENTERTAINMENT COMPLEX, LEVEL 14, EAST TOWER DISTRICT. FIRST DRINK COMPLIMENTARY.

A QR code pulses at the bottom of the projection. Scanning it would add the Neon Court's contact channel to your datapad. Their recruitment is subtler than the Iron Collective's: no pamphlets, no speeches. Just the promise of a better night, and the implication that enough better nights add up to a better life.

You notice that the advertisement's projection zone has a small interactive component. A figure in the hologram turns toward you, makes eye contact, and winks. Targeted advertising. It saw you looking and responded.

choices.text: Scan the QR code
Ignore it. Pretty lies are still lies.
Head to the drone in the doorway

node_035.title: Neon Court Contact
text: Your datapad buzzes as the Neon Court channel installs itself. The interface is sleek, dark-themed, with a logo that shifts colors like oil on water. A welcome message populates immediately.
WELCOME TO THE COURT. YOU'VE TAKEN THE FIRST STEP TOWARD A LIFE LESS ORDINARY. YOUR COMPLIMENTARY INVITATION HAS BEEN LOGGED. PRESENT THIS CODE AT ANY NEON COURT ESTABLISHMENT FOR VIP ACCESS.

Below the message, a secondary notification appears, smaller, private. NOT THE OFFICIAL CHANNEL. SOMEONE ADDED A PIGGYBACK MESSAGE. It reads: WE KNOW ABOUT THE MAN IN THE COAT. IF YOU WANT TO KNOW WHO SENT HIM, COME TO THE SILK ROAD LOUNGE, LEVEL 14. ASK FOR MIRROR. BRING SOMETHING TO TRADE.
Someone inside the Neon Court knows about the man looking for you. The question is whether that makes them an ally or just another predator with better manners.

choices.text: Interesting. Check the drone in the doorway too.
You've gathered enough leads. Time to regroup.

node_036.title: The Ghost Drone
text: You approach the doorway casually, as if you're just stepping out of the rain. The drone hovers at knee height, no bigger than your fist, nearly silent. Its chassis is matte black and featureless, no manufacturer's mark, no serial number, no identifying features of any kind. Custom built. Ghost Network hardware.

The green light blinks faster as you get close. When you're within arm's reach, the drone tilts, exposing its underside where a data chip is attached with magnetic tape. A tiny speaker emits a single word, synthesized and genderless: "Take."
The data chip is identical to the one Mako showed you, if she showed you one. Matte black, no markings. The Ghost Network's calling card.

choices.text: Take the data chip
[Tech 12] Try to trace the drone's signal back to its operator
Leave it. Too many strangers handing you things today.

node_037.title: The Network's Gift
text: You pull the chip free. The drone's light turns solid green, then dies. The drone drops into your hand, inert, its purpose fulfilled. It's lighter than you expected, barely there, like holding a thought.

The chip is warm against your fingers. Whatever data it holds is waiting for an air-gapped device, away from the district network's surveillance. Your room has an old terminal that might serve, if it still works.

The Ghost Network. They've been watching you. They left you messages and data chips and sent anonymous warnings. In a district where trust is the scarcest resource, they're extending something that looks like faith. Or recruitment. In the Lower District, the difference is academic.
As the drone dies in your hand, a final message appears on its tiny screen: YOU HAVE FRIENDS YOU HAVEN'T MET YET. WHEN THE 48 HOURS ARE UP, FIND US OR WE'LL FIND YOU.

choices.text: Pocket the chip and the dead drone. Both might be useful.
Check out the Iron Collective recruiter before leaving

node_038.title: Tracing the Signal
text: You pull out your datapad and run a quick signal trace on the drone's communication frequency before taking the chip. The drone is broadcasting on a channel so heavily encrypted that your datapad's processing power barely scratches the surface, but you manage to catch a fragment before the signal hops to a new frequency.

The drone is being controlled from somewhere within a three-block radius. The signal bounced off four relay points before reaching the drone, but the origination pattern suggests a location above street level, fourth or fifth floor. And the encryption protocol is nothing you've seen before. It's not corporate. It's not military. It's something else entirely, hand-coded, elegant, and paranoid.
Whoever the Ghost Network is, they have technical capabilities that rival or exceed the corporate security apparatus. And they're operating right here, in the Lower District, hiding in plain sight.
The chip falls into your hand as you finish the trace. The drone goes dark.

choices.text: Impressive. Pocket everything and plan your next move.

node_039.title: Taking Stock
text: You step back under an awning and let the rain curtain the world for a moment. Time to take stock.

This morning you woke up in a box in the Lower District with nothing but a knife and a handful of credits. Now you have a deadline, a warning, factions circling you like sharks around a raft, and more questions than answers. Someone from the towers is hunting you for reasons connected to something called LATENCY. The Ghost Network is watching you and leaving gifts. The Iron Collective is recruiting muscle. The Neon Court knows more than they should. The Ashen Circle is preaching against the very technology that might save or enslave you.

And underneath all of it, like a splinter working its way to the surface, is the question Mako raised: who put you in the Lower District in the first place? Who scrubbed your records? Who made you invisible, and why has someone now decided to make you visible again?
The rain intensifies. The patrol drones bank and regroup overhead. The city goes on, indifferent to the small drama of one person standing at the intersection of too many dangerous roads.
But you're standing. And you have choices. And in the Lower District, that counts for more than it should.

choices.text: Head back to your room. You need to read those data chips in private.
No time to rest. Follow up on the job you accepted.
Find the Ashen Circle's sunset service. The preacher said they have answers.

node_040.title: Prologue's End
text: You move through the rain with purpose now. The Lower District flows around you, a river of neon light and human desperation, but you're no longer just floating in the current. You have a direction, even if you can't see the destination yet.

Above you, the Cloud Towers pulse with their slow, patient rhythm, broadcasting the signal that keeps the immortal immortal and the rest of the city locked in its place. Somewhere in those towers, someone has your name on a list and a team ready to collect. Somewhere in these streets, other forces are gathering around you, offering tools and weapons and alliances that all come with their own price tags.

The game has started. You didn't choose to play, but the stakes are your life and possibly something larger than your life, something connected to a word that keeps surfacing like a body in deep water: LATENCY.
Whatever LATENCY is, whatever it means, the next forty-eight hours will determine whether you uncover the truth or become another casualty of it. The prologue is over. The story begins.

text.text: Your HUD flickers and for a fraction of a second, a string of code scrolls across your vision. You don't recognize the language, but somewhere in your augmented brain, a subroutine activates that you didn't know you had. A clock. Counting down. 47:59:58. 47:59:57. 47:59:56.

Deep in your core processes, below the emotion layer, below the personality matrix, a dormant file activates. It has no name, only a timestamp from before your earliest memory. It contains a single instruction: SURVIVE. REMEMBER. FIND THE SOURCE.

The rain runs down your tusks and drips from your jaw. Your ancestors survived the Gene Riots. Your grandparents survived the Relocation. Your parents survived the Lower District. Survival is encoded in your blood, in every cell that the corporations couldn't patent or control. Whatever comes next, you will endure it. You always have.

You feel the centuries in your bones, the long memory of your people whispering that empires rise and fall, that towers crumble, that no power lasts forever. The corporations believe they have conquered death. The elves know that death is patient, and that patient things always win in the end.

Baseline. Unremarkable. Invisible. They keep underestimating the ordinary, the ones without augmentations or genetic legacies or corporate backing. They forget that every revolution in human history was started by someone with nothing to lose and everything to prove. You have nothing. You have everything.
